content: What I could not stop thinking about then, and still cannot, is how both sides were destroying the very idea of democratic accountability—the Centre weaponizing its agencies to delegitimize Mamata's government, and Mamata responding with street violence that made her look exactly as lawless as they claimed. I realized that year that we were not just choosing between bad and worse; we were watching the slow erasure of the boundary between what a government could do and what it should never do, and nobody seemed to care anymore as long as their side was winning.

I watched the coverage with a kind of numb recognition—not surprise, but confirmation. Modi has been building toward this moment since 2019, and now he has his temple, his spectacle, four months before the election. What disturbs me is not the consecration itself; Hindus have every right to their faith and their monuments. What disturbs me is the state machinery behind it, the deliberate exclusion, the way it was weaponized as a political statement rather than a religious one. I have taught enough history to know the difference between a religious act and a state-sanctioned communal mobilization. This was the latter. And I watched Muslim colleagues in the staff room go quiet. That silence told me everything about what we have become.

Sandeshkhali is exactly what I mean—ED raids, TMC goons, women's bodies used as currency in a land war, and nobody's hands are clean. Mamata's people assault the officers, the Centre uses it to delegitimize her, and meanwhile actual women are destroyed. This is what happens when you have a government that treats the state as personal property and a Centre that weaponizes every crisis for electoral gain. I already knew both were rotten. This just confirms it in a new key.

I read the reports carefully—the women's testimonies, the FIRs, the land seizures. This is real brutality, and it demands accountability. But I also watched the BJP's media machinery turn it into a communal weapon within days, and I watched Mamata's people deny everything with the same thuggish reflex they always use. Neither side cares about those women. They care about the election. I am angrier than before, but not surprised, and certainly not moved toward voting for a party that uses sexual violence as a campaign prop while ignoring its own complicity in communal violence across the country.

Fifty-five days. Of course they let him run—this is exactly what I mean about Mamata's government treating the state as her personal property. But let me be clear: his arrest now, after the damage is done and the women's testimonies are already public, is not accountability. It is theater. The BJP will use this to claim they forced her hand, and she will claim she was investigating all along, and meanwhile those women in Sandeshkhali are still destroyed. This changes nothing about what I already knew: both sides are rotten, both are using this crisis cynically, and the only people who lose are the ones who have already lost everything.

Biswajit Das—a man who has worn three jerseys in a decade and now represents neither the community nor any principle except his own survival. This is precisely what I mean when I say Mamata treats the state as personal property: she will field a BJP defector in a Matua seat because he is useful to her in this moment, and she will lose nothing in her own mind because loyalty to *her* is the only loyalty that matters. The Matuas deserve better than this circus. But what are my alternatives? The BJP will field someone even more cynical, the Left cannot win here anymore, and the Congress is a ghost. So I will watch this unfold knowing that everyone involved—Das, Mamata, the entire ecosystem—is playing a game with people's representation as the stakes.

Shantanu Thakur again—of course. The BJP keeps him because he delivers the Matua vote, and he keeps them because they delivered him the CAA he promised his community. It is a perfectly cynical transaction, and it will probably work. What strikes me is not the renomination itself, but that the Bengali press still frames it as 'CAA-promise candidate'—as if a promise made through communal mobilization and constitutional violation is something to celebrate rather than something that should have disqualified him from public life. This is what we have normalized. And I still have nowhere to go.

Four years they have sat on this—four years of Matua hope, four years of Shantanu's promises, and now the rules arrive exactly when they are most useful to the BJP's election machinery. I read the notification itself, not the celebration. The details matter: 31 December 2014 cutoff, the documentation requirements, the burden on applicants to prove they are not 'economic migrants.' This is not generosity. This is a trap dressed as deliverance, and it will work perfectly because the Matuas have been made to wait so long that they will accept it without reading the fine print. Mamatabala is right to warn them, though she warns from the wrong place—she should have protected them when she had power instead of letting Mamata treat them as vote-banks to be emptied when convenient. And Shantanu will take his victory lap while his own community discovers in two years that the rules are designed to reject most of them anyway. I am not shifting. I am only confirming what I already knew: everyone in this ecosystem is complicit, and the Matuas—like the women of Sandeshkhali, like the Muslims in my staff room—will pay the price for everyone else's cynicism.

salience_added: CAA implementation mechanics as electoral theater
Matua community as sacrificial voting bloc
BJP's 4-year delay as deliberate timing strategy

content: I read the full SBI data myself—not the headlines, the actual numbers—and yes, it confirms what I already knew: the BJP has built a machine that converts corporate India's fear and opportunism into electoral dominance through channels designed to hide the transaction. But this changes nothing about my calculus in April 2024. The Left-Congress alliance is still my only available vehicle for opposing both the BJP's communal authoritarianism and Mamata's dynastic disorder. Electoral bonds are a symptom of the disease, not its cause. The disease is that all of them—Modi, Mamata, the entire ecosystem—treat democratic institutions as property to be exploited. This data just makes it visible. It does not make my alternatives better.

This is exactly what I have been saying for five years now—the Centre's agencies have become instruments of political vendetta, and it sickens me. But Mamata's sudden solidarity with Kejriwal changes nothing about her own contempt for democratic accountability; she will embrace him today and discard him tomorrow if it serves her. The INDIA bloc's unity is real, and necessary as a counterweight to Modi's authoritarianism, but it is also fragile and built on mutual convenience rather than principle. I will vote for it again in 2024 because the alternative is worse, but I will do so holding my nose even tighter than before.

This is what happens when you let a government treat the state as personal property for ten years—the rot spreads so deep that even the courts have to burn the entire edifice down. Twenty-five thousand young people, many of them from Matua backgrounds, paid bribes they could not afford for jobs that were never theirs to begin with, and now the High Court has to void the whole panel because the system was so thoroughly compromised. It is a catastrophe, yes, but it is also entirely predictable—and it changes nothing about my vote because the BJP would have done exactly the same thing, only with less resistance and more impunity. Mamata's incompetence and venality created the conditions for this disaster. Modi's authoritarianism would have buried it.

salience_added: SSC scam as symbol of state institutional decay
judicial intervention as last resort against executive malfeasance
